current_fact.fact_text: On March 25, 1995, Ward Cunningham made the WikiWikiWeb public, creating the first true wiki—an editable website where anyone could modify pages directly in the browser. The name itself came from the Hawaiian word for “quick,” and the concept quietly rewired how humans collaborate, laying the groundwork for everything from fan communities to Wikipedia.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

current_fact.match_reason: The birth of the first wiki, an early social web tool, pairs with an episode reflecting on our digital lives, attention, and technological dependence.

queue.fact_text: On March 25, 2006, protesters in Minsk clashed with riot police after Belarus’s presidential election, widely condemned as rigged. Among those arrested was opposition leader Alyaksandr Kazulin, whose detention symbolized the high personal cost of challenging an entrenched authoritarian regime.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

queue.match_reason: Protesters risking arrest to demand fair elections align with an assertive, outspoken hip-hop episode that channels resistance and speaking out against power.

queue.fact_text: On the same day in 2006, a lone gunman opened fire at a house party in Seattle’s Capitol Hill neighborhood, killing six people before taking his own life. The tragedy highlighted how spaces meant for music, art, and community can be violently disrupted, leaving a lasting scar on a city’s cultural memory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A heartbreaking community tragedy is matched with a reflective, healing-focused episode that holds space for grief, recovery, and slow mending.

queue.fact_text: On March 25, 2018, shortly after Turkey’s Afrin offensive captured the city in northwestern Syria, the Syrian Democratic Forces began an insurgency against the new occupation. The struggle in Afrin turned from open battle into a shadow war of ambushes and resistance, in a region already marked by years of civil conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: An insurgency in a contested, semi-arid region of the Middle East links with an episode about desert peoples, exile, and musical forms of resistance.

queue.fact_text: On March 25, 2006 in Seattle, while one tragedy unfolded, another corner of the world was wrestling with its own: Belarus’s contested election and street protests. These parallel events, separated by thousands of kilometers, show how a single date can hold both intimate and political ruptures in different places at once.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: This meta-fact about simultaneous global events sits with a drifting, transnational sailing episode, emphasizing how many parallel stories share the same dates and seas.

queue.fact_text: On March 25, 1948 in Oklahoma, in 1979 at Cape Canaveral, and in 1995 on a quiet web server, humans made three very different bets on the future: predicting tornadoes, reusing spacecraft, and letting strangers edit the same page. Together they sketch a pattern of curiosity and risk, stretching from storms and rockets to code and community.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A composite reflection on multiple March 25 breakthroughs mirrors Sedna FM’s own "where it all began" origin episode, tying together beginnings in science, space, and the web with the station’s first broadcast.

queue.fact_text: Across centuries, March 25 has seen empires fall, moons discovered, storms predicted, and anonymous coders invited into the same editing room. The date itself becomes a quiet reminder that history is less a straight line and more a spiral—repeating themes of risk, resistance, creativity, and change in endlessly new forms.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A broad, reflective observation about the date as a whole fits with an introductory, open-ended Evening Flow designed as a journey without strict rules.

queue.fact_text: Some historians note that Bach’s shining "morning star" cantata, Huygens’ glimpse of Titan, and the launch-prepared Columbia all share a similar impulse: to frame the unknown—whether divine, celestial, or technological—inside structures we can inhabit, from music to math to metal. March 25 becomes a small, recurring window where humans look up and try again.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: A cosmic, contemplative link between music, moons, and machines pairs naturally with a "Cosmic"-themed episode about futuristic rhythms and gradual transformation.

published.fact_text: In 1655, using a homemade telescope with a lens ground by hand, Dutch scientist Christiaan Huygens spotted a tiny "star" near Saturn that turned out to be Titan, its largest moon. At 5,150 km across, Titan is bigger than the planet Mercury and remains one of the most Earth-like worlds in our Solar System, with thick orange skies and liquid hydrocarbon lakes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal space discovery pairs with an episode themed around "space" and creative emptiness, echoing Titan’s vast, mysterious atmosphere.

published.fact_text: On March 25, 1979, the Space Shuttle Columbia became the first fully operational orbiter delivered to Kennedy Space Center. It marked a radical shift in spaceflight: a partially reusable spaceplane that promised to turn launches from rare spectacles into something closer to a routine transport system.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: A milestone in human journeys beyond Earth fits with an episode framed as a collective voyage through sound toward Sedna.

published.fact_text: In 1948, meteorologists at Tinker Air Force Base in Oklahoma issued the first successful tornado forecast, predicting a tornado that did in fact strike the base later that day. It was a controversial act at the time—tornado prediction was considered too uncertain and even panic-inducing—yet it opened the door to the modern tornado warning systems that save lives today.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A story about confronting violent weather connects naturally with an episode built around rain, storms, and the hope that they eventually pass.

published.fact_text: On March 25, 1821, Greeks mark the traditional beginning of their War of Independence against the Ottoman Empire, even though fighting had already started a month earlier. The chosen date intertwined rebellion with religious symbolism, aligning the uprising with the Feast of the Annunciation and turning a political struggle into a sacred national narrative.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A pivotal Greek Mediterranean uprising pairs with a sea-themed episode that sails through Greek and neighboring coastal sounds.

published.fact_text: In 1725, Johann Sebastian Bach’s chorale cantata "Wie schön leuchtet der Morgenstern" (BWV 1) was first performed on the Feast of the Annunciation, which that year coincided with Palm Sunday. The piece weaves a Lutheran hymn into a luminous tapestry for choir and orchestra, turning a single congregational song into an intricate universe of sound.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A radiant "morning star" cantata for a spring feast resonates with a slow, sunlit episode about luminous mornings and reflective calm.

published.fact_text: On March 25, 1576, actor and entrepreneur Jerome Savage took out a sub-lease to open the Newington Butts Theatre just outside London’s city boundaries. By building beyond the strict jurisdiction of the City, early theatre-makers created a loophole that helped birth the public playhouse culture which later nurtured Shakespeare and his contemporaries.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

published.match_reason: An early London theatre experiment outside the city walls pairs with a modern sonic walk through London’s creative streets.

published.fact_text: In 1584, Queen Elizabeth I granted Sir Walter Raleigh a patent to establish colonies in the region the English called Virginia. His failed Roanoke experiments still haunt history with the mystery of the "Lost Colony," where more than 100 settlers vanished leaving only a cryptic carving: CROATOAN.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A story of risky voyages and new worlds fits with an episode about travel, dislocation, and the perspective shifts of moving through unfamiliar places.

published.fact_text: On March 25, 1519, Hernán Cortés fought and defeated the Indigenous Tabascan forces in present-day Tabasco, Mexico. After the battle, local leaders offered gifts, including 20 enslaved women—one of them, Malintzin (La Malinche), would become Cortés’ translator and cultural bridge, profoundly shaping the conquest of the Aztec Empire.

episode.title: Morning Drops #26 - Canserbero
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the story of Canserbero, the Venezuelan rapper who reshaped Latin American hip hop with his raw voice and deeply reflective lyrics. A journey into his music, his vision, and the legacy of an artist who transformed personal and social struggles into powerful storytelling.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como una película y su tragedia, comedia y ficción

published.match_reason: A pivotal, violent moment in Latin American history is paired with an episode centered on Latin American hip hop that critically reflects on power, history, and identity.

published.fact_text: In 1776, during the American Revolutionary War, Patriot forces raided Tybee Island off the coast of Georgia—not to attack British soldiers, but primarily to recapture enslaved people who had escaped to British protection. The episode reveals a lesser-known tension of the revolution: a struggle for liberty that often excluded those held in bondage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: The contradiction between proclaimed freedom and ongoing oppression suits a politically charged episode about music that calls out systemic injustice.

published.fact_text: On March 25, 1708, a French invasion fleet anchored off Fife Ness on the Scottish coast, hoping to land troops and restore the exiled James Francis Edward Stuart to the British throne. Storms, poor coordination, and the Royal Navy’s pursuit turned the operation into an abortive ghost-invasion that never properly reached shore.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

published.match_reason: A thwarted invasion off the Scottish coast matches an episode explicitly set "under Scottish skies," grounding history in the same landscape.

published.fact_text: In the year 1000, the Fatimid caliph al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah had his powerful eunuch vizier Barjawan assassinated and personally assumed control of the government. Al-Hakim’s later rule would earn him a reputation as one of history’s most enigmatic and eccentric monarchs, alternating between generous patronage and startling decrees.

episode.title: Morning Drops #02 - Waves of the Maghreb
episode.description: Oriental vibes guided by Yasmin. Discovering curious facts about the Maghreb, together with its fascinating music! A wonderful trip with a selection of songs from North Africa.
songs: Nass El Ghiwane - El Madi Fate

published.match_reason: A North African caliphate’s internal power shift aligns with an episode that explores Maghreb history and soundscapes.

published.fact_text: On March 25, 1065, the Great German Pilgrimage to Jerusalem was ambushed by Bedouin bandits on Good Friday, suffering heavy casualties in the desert. The attack showed how even acts of peaceful devotion could collide with the harsh realities of long-distance travel across contested and unforgiving landscapes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A perilous spiritual journey through dangerous terrain mirrors an intense, nature-focused voyage into Arctic waters in search of whales.

published.fact_text: In 410, the fortress capital of Guanggu, seat of the Southern Yan state, fell to the Jin dynasty general Liu Yu. The collapse of Southern Yan is one of many short-lived dynastic chapters in Chinese history, a reminder that even seemingly solid realms can vanish within a single campaign season.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A story of rise and fall in a turbulent era is balanced by an episode that intentionally slows time down, offering a contemplative counterpoint to imperial collapse.

published.fact_text: On March 25, 1941, the Kingdom of Yugoslavia signed the Tripartite Pact, formally joining the Axis powers—only for a popular and military coup to overturn the decision just two days later. The brief alliance illustrates how quickly geopolitical alignments can flip when public sentiment and power structures collide.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: Rapid political reversals and popular resistance in Yugoslavia resonate with an episode devoted to a fiercely political artist who scrutinizes power and identity.

published.fact_text: On March 25, 1949, the Soviet Union launched Operation Priboi, deporting more than 92,000 people—labeled "kulaks" and other "enemies"—from the Baltic states to remote regions of Siberia in just a few days. Entire families were taken in night raids, their lives compressed into cattle cars and a single suitcase.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

published.match_reason: A mass deportation from Eastern Europe is paired with an episode traveling through nearby Polish and Slavic landscapes, hinting at the region’s heavy historical shadows beneath today’s journeys.

published.fact_text: On March 25, 1971, South Vietnamese forces abandoned Operation Lam Son 719, a major attempt to cut the Ho Chi Minh trail in Laos. The operation’s retreat exposed the difficulties of “Vietnamization” and showed how jungle geography, logistics, and morale can matter as much as raw firepower.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: A fraught Southeast Asian military campaign is juxtaposed with an episode set in peaceful, contemporary Thailand, transforming a region once defined by war into a space of discipline and healing movement.

published.fact_text: On March 25, 1995, Ward Cunningham made the WikiWikiWeb public, creating the first true wiki—an editable website where anyone could modify pages directly in the browser. The name itself came from the Hawaiian word for “quick,” and the concept quietly rewired how humans collaborate, laying the groundwork for everything from fan communities to Wikipedia.

episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.
